008
009                                                  A BSTRACT
010
011            As the space of causal sequence modeling architectures continues to grow, the
012            need to develop a general framework for their analysis becomes increasingly im-
013            portant. With this aim, we draw insights from classical signal processing and
014            control theory, to develop a quantitative measure of memory utilization: the inter-
015            nal mechanisms through which a model stores past information to produce future
016            outputs. This metric, which we call effective state-size (ESS), is tailored to the
017            fundamental class of systems with input-invariant and input-varying linear oper-
018            ators, encompassing a variety of computational units such as variants of attention,
019
               convolutions, and recurrences. Unlike prior work on memory utilization, which
               either relies on raw operator visualizations (e.g. attention maps), or simply the
020
               total memory capacity (i.e. cache size) of a model, our metrics provide highly
021
               interpretable and actionable measurements. In particular, we show how ESS can
022            be leveraged to improve initialization strategies, inform novel regularizers and
023            advance the performance-efficiency frontier through model distillation. Further-
024            more, we demonstrate that the effect of context delimiters (such as end-of-speech
025            tokens) on ESS highlights cross-architectural differences in how large language
026            models utilize their available memory to recall information. Overall, we find that
027            ESS provides valuable insights into the dynamics that dictate memory utilization,
028            enabling the design of more efficient and effective sequence models.
029
030
031   1    I NTRODUCTION
032
033   In recent years, the success of autoregressive sequence modeling in the context of deep learning has
034   largely been driven by advancements in highly parallelizable causal architectures, such as the trans-
035
      former (Vaswani et al., 2023). However, despite their strong performance and hardware efficiency,
      understanding the inner workings of these neural networks remains a challenging task due to their
036
      non-linearity and the diversity of fundamental building blocks used. To this end, we leverage a new
037
      class of model abstractions, allowing for the development of a unified framework for the analysis of
038   these computational units.
039
040   In particular, we note that the majority of sequence models of practical interest can formally be
041
      expressed as either linear systems (y = T u) or systems with input-varying linear operators
      (y = fT (u)u), the latter of which we abbreviate as LIV1 . LIVs generalize the notion of adap-
042
      tive, or data-controlled operators to a broader class than previously described in Massaroli et al.
043
      (2021); Poli et al. (2023). The input-varying linear operator framework decouples the input-varying
044   featurization u 7→ T := fT (u) and the linear mapping y = T u required to construct and apply the
045   operator respectively. This decomposition enables a wide array of deep learning primitives to be
046   uniformly formulated as linear systems, including models like convolutions [40; 47; 35; 56], linear
047   state-transition recurrences [15; 18; 27; 61; 29; 26; 75; 49; 16], and attention variants [68; 34; 65].
048
      Current approaches to analyzing the inner workings of LIVs often rely upon simple visualizations of
049
      the materialized operator T (or the aggregation of T across multiple layers and residuals) [45; 69; 1;
050   4; 74; 63]. However, these visualizations alone often fail to highlight critical properties that explain
051
052       1
            We abbreviate them as LIVs instead of IVLs to conform to the classical convention, where linear time-
053   varying and time-invariant systems are abbreviated as LTVs and LTIs respectively, as well as recent works such
      as Zancato et al. (2024), which call them “linear input-varying systems”.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                                                                      Sequence Index
                                                                                                Analysis

064
065    Figure 1: An overview of the effective state-size metric and its various downstream applications.
066
067   how different models construct internal representations of the input data. Moreover, prior attempts in
068   obtaining quantitative metrics, such as through spectral analysis of the operator T (Min & Li, 2024;
069   Bhojanapalli et al., 2020), are either limited to a specific model class or do not appropriately take
070
      into account important conflating factors like the causal masking of T which significantly distorts
      the metric (Wu et al., 2024).
071
072   In this work, we focus our analysis on the working memory2 of model architectures and examine
073   two aspects of model memory in particular: memory capacity (i.e. cache/state size) and memory
074   utilization. Notably, memory capacity alone can be misleading, as models with similar capacities
075   may learn to utilize their available memory to varying degrees. Therefore, we introduce the no-
076
      tion of memory utilization – a measure that provides deeper insight into the differences between
      architectures with comparable computational efficiency.
077
078   Our main technical contributions can be summarized as follows:
079        • We draw from classical signal processing and control theory, and propose effective state-
080           size (ESS) – a metric computed by taking the rank of Ti:,:i−1 – as a proxy for memory
081           utilization in LIVs (Section 3).
082        • We validate ESS beyond its theoretical interpretation by demonstrating its correlation with
083
              performance across a wide range of models and memory-intensive synthetic tasks, includ-
              ing associative recall and selective copying (Section 4).
084
           • We construct initializers motivated by ESS which result in performance improvements rel-
085
              ative to default initializers (Section 5.1).
086        • We explore the use of the ESS metric as a means of enhancing the performance-efficiency
087           trade-off by demonstrating its ability to inform model distillation (Section 5.2).
088        • We extend the utility of ESS to language, demonstrating how it captures a previously un-
089           characterized property of LLMs: state modulation (Section 5.3). This phenomenon pro-
090           vides concrete intuition as to why introducing input dependencies into state transitions is
091           crucial for in-context recall.
092
093   2     R ELATED W ORK
094
095   In this section, we briefly describe previous works that are closely related to the core findings of the
096   paper. For an extended discussion of the related works, refer to Section B.
097
098   Classifying sequence models. A sequence model can be defined as a mapping from input se-
099   quences to output sequences that leverages a state to maintain information across time. Within the
100   scope of deep learning, the early attempts at constructing sequence models fall under the class of
101   non-linear RNNs (Rumelhart & McClelland, 1987; Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) which ex-
102   hibit non-linear state space dynamics. However, in recent years, models with linear state transitions
103   have grown in popularity due to inherent trainability (Pascanu et al., 2013) and efficiency (Martin &
104
      Cundy, 2018) limitations in the non-linear regime. These models fall under two categories: linear
      systems and systems with input-varying linear operators (LIVs). We note that linear systems can
105
106      2
           Here, we refer to “memory” in the sense commonly associated with the “state” of dynamical systems, as
107   described by Willems (1989), as opposed to the notion of language models memorizing some fact encountered
      during training (Allen-Zhu & Li, 2024).


                                                          2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
      further be broken down into time-invariant (LTI) and time-varying (LTV) systems, depending on
109   whether or not the parameters of the realized recurrence change as a function of time (i.e. sequence
110   index). This taxonomy has also been used in prior work (Zancato et al., 2024) to motivate the con-
111   struction of novel architectures. In modern deep learning, LTI systems (Lindquist & Picci, 1979;
112   Ljung, 1999; Ho & Kalman, 1966) can be found in frameworks like S4 (Gu et al., 2022a). However,
113   given the lack of expressivity afforded to LTIs, the current state-of-the-art models fall under the more
114   expressive LIV class (Gu & Dao, 2024; Poli et al., 2023; Vaswani et al., 2023) which parameterizes
115   the state-space dynamics as a function of the input. Since LIVs constitute the minimal superclass
116   containing most sequence models of practical interest, the goal of this work is to develop a quan-
117
      titative means of analyzing models under the LIV abstraction. To do so, we draw from minimal
      realization theory (DeWilde & van der Veen, 1998; Akaike, 1974), which has previously been ap-
118
      plied within the scope of classical linear systems but has yet to be extended to modern LIV sequence
119
      models.
120
121
      Quantifying memory utilization in sequence models. As discussed in Section 1, the current
122   landscape of examining memory in sequence models primarily reduces to qualitative measures or
123   limited quantitative ones. Here, we expand on the latter, noting that commonly used measures such
124   as model size (Hoffmann et al., 2022) and cache/state size (which we define formally as theoretically
125   realizable state-size in Section 3) are imperfect means of measuring memory in sequence models.
126   Namely, while these metrics serve as reasonable proxies for the capacity of a model to learn, they
127   fail to capture how much of that capacity is realized. As such, they ignore important aspects of the
128   model pipeline such as data, initialization, and optimization. To capture these aspects of network
129   training, we apply the notions of numerical and effective rank (Roy & Vetterli, 2007) to the minimal
130
      realization problem, which gives rise to the ESS metric. We elaborate on this in Section 3.
131   We highlight the concept of semiseparable rank (Vandebril et al., 2005; Xia et al., 2010), which has
132   been recently explored in Dao & Gu (2024). Similar to ESS, semiseparable rank examines the rank
133   of specific submatrices of T . However, as semiseparable rank is primarily motivated by the design of
134   efficient algorithms, we find it most closely aligns with the concept of theoretically realizable state
135   size (TSS) in the context of our work. In this study, we provide complete derivations of ESS, clearly
136
      distinguish it from TSS (and by extension semiseparable rank), and demonstrate its effectiveness as
      a proxy for memory utilization in practical settings for modern LIV sequence models.
137
138
139   3   T HEORY
140
141   In this section, we begin by showing that most modern sequence models can effectively materialize
142   a linear operator T . We then formally define ESS as a metric derived from T , providing theoretical
143   insights grounded in minimal realization theory. Finally, we detail the practical computation of ESS,
144   proposing two variants: tolerance-ESS and entropy-ESS.
145   Using the flattened notation, we let T ∈ Rdℓ×dℓ , u, y ∈ Rdℓ denote the operator, inputs, and outputs
146   respectively, ℓ denote the sequence length and d denote the channel dimension. Here, we index
147   sequence indices with subscripts, i.e. Tij ∈ Rd×d , ui ∈ Rd and channels (and other non-temporal
148   dimensions) with superscripts, i.e. T αβ ∈ Rℓ×ℓ , uα ∈ Rℓ . For additional details on notation, refer
149   to Section C.1.
150
151   A unified representation of sequence models. While typically nonlinear, most sequence models
152   of interest can effectively materialize a linear operator T , where the equation y = T u faithfully
153   expresses the computation performed by the model (see Section D.2 for further elaboration):
154        Tij = Ci Bj     linear attention,          Tij = Ci Ai−1 · · · Aj+1 Bj           recurrence,
155
           Tij = Ki−j          convolution,           Tij = Ci Ki−j Bj               gated convolution,
156
           Tij = σ(Ci Bj )        attention.
157
158   We make a distinction between linear systems (such as convolutions) and LIVs (such as attention
159   and gated convolutions). In the former, the operator T is input-invariant whereas the latter are
160   constructed via causal featurizers that map past inputs into features, i.e. fB : u:i 7→ Bi , which are
161   then used to construct the elements of T as outlined above. We begin our formulation by restricting
      the scope to linear systems.


                                                         3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


162
      Operator-recurrence duality and the minimal recurrent realization of linear systems. Con-
163   sider a general linear recurrence formulated as follows:
164
                                        si+1 = Ai si + Bi ui ,     yi = Ci si + Di ui ,                          (1)
165
                           ni+1 ×ni            ni+1 ×d            d×ni          d×d
166   where (Ai ∈ R              , Bi ∈ R        , Ci ∈         R      , Di ∈ R     )i∈[ℓ] ; si and ni are the state
167   and state-size at sequence index i respectively. Classic results (DeWilde & van der Veen, 1998,
168
      ch. 3) establish that for any given input-invariant operator T (i.e. linear system), there exist infinite
      recurrent realizations in the form of Equation (1), motivating the search for the minimal one.
169
170    Theorem 3.1. Given any causal input-invariant operator T , there exist infinite variations of linear
171    recurrences in the form of Equation (1) that realize an equivalent input-output operator.
172   A simple extension of the proof of this theorem (in Section C.2.4) demonstrates the following:
173
       Theorem 3.2. The rank of the operator submatrix (Hi ≡ Ti:,:i−1 ) determines the minimal state
174
       size required to represent the causal operation (y = T u) as a recurrence.
175
176   Refer to Section C.3 for the proof.
177   Note that since we use the flattened notation, where T ∈ Rdℓ×dℓ , rank(Hi ) determines the minimal
178   state-size required for processing all d channels in a layer via a recurrence at the ith index in the
179   sequence. We formally refer to this metric as per-sequence index effective state-size (ESS) and
180   discuss its interpretation for both linear systems and LIVs below.
181
182   Interpreting effective state-size. As shown in Theorem 3.2, the ESS of an input-invariant linear
183   system is given by its minimal state-size which is directly interpretable as a measure of model
184   memory utilization. For LIVs, however, ESS is also a function of the input (rank(fT (u)i:,:i−1 ))
185
      which means that the minimal realization process outlined in the proof of Theorem 3.1 is no longer
      guaranteed to obtain recurrences that preserve causality (i.e. the minimally realized features A∗i
186
      depend on future inputs uk , k > i)3 . Nevertheless, ESS lower bounds the state-size ni (refer to
187
      Section C.2.2), meaning that for any LIV, an equivalent recurrence must necessarily materialize a
188
      state-size at least as large as its ESS. Therefore, we claim that ESS serves as a proxy for memory
189   utilization in not only linear systems, but in LIVs as well. We empirically validate the usefulness of
190   this interpretation of ESS for LIVs in Sections 4 and 5.
191
192   Memory capacity in LIVs. The memory capacity of LIVs is given by the state-size ni . We
193   formally refer to it as theoretically realizable state-size (TSS), as it serves as a tight upper bound
194   for ESS. For models without realization-agnostic minimal recurrent formulations, such as softmax
195   attention, we resort to the trivial realization as shown in Equation C.2.5, in which TSS (for a single
196   channel) is equal to the sequence index i. For models like SSMs and linear attention variants, TSS
197   is equal to the state-size defined by their recurrent formulation. We refer readers to Section D.2
198
      for detailed operator-specific derivations of TSS. Recall that ESS depends not only on the model’s
      functional form, but also on the input data, optimization, and more generally anything that impacts
199
      the realization of T ; in contrast, TSS is limited in that it is determined solely by the model’s structure.
200
201
      3.1      C OMPUTING E FFECTIVE S TATE -S IZE
202
203   In practice, computing ESS requires a few additional considerations due to the numerical errors and
204   approximations involved in computing the rank of matrices. We propose two approaches – both
205   of which rely on singular values (Σi ) from taking the singular value decomposition (SVD) of Hi
206   (which equals fT (u)i:,:i−1 for LIVs) – that provide complementary perspectives on the same metric.
207
208   Tolerance-ESS. Here, a tolerance value τ is manually selected to threshold the singular values of
209   Hi , determining the tolerance-ESS metric as follows:
210     tolerance-ESS(Hi , τ ) := |{σim : σim > τ }|, where σim ∈ Σi , Ui diag(Σi )Vi = SVD(Hi ). (2)
211
      According to the Eckart–Young–Mirsky theorem, the tolerance-ESS metric can be interpreted as the
212   minimum state size necessary for an input-invariant recurrence to approximate the original operator,
213   such that the spectral norm of the approximation error remains below the specified tolerance level
214   (||Tij − Tij∗ ||2 ≤ τ ).
215
         3
             An example of a causality preserving realization of LIVs is the trivial realization shown in Equation (C.2.5).


                                                                4
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


216
      Entropy-ESS. One drawback of tolerance-ESS is its reliance on the somewhat arbitrary selection
217   of a tolerance value. One can instead compute the effective rank (Roy & Vetterli, 2007), which
218   involves exponentiating the normalized spectral entropy (perplexity) of Hi :
219                                           X                                      σim
220                 entropy-ESS(Hi ) := exp −         pm
                                                       i log(p m
                                                               i )  , where  pm
                                                                              i   =        .     (3)
221                                               m
                                                                                    ∥σi ∥1
222
      Entropy-ESS is particularly useful for summarizing metrics across the entire tolerance space,
223
      whereas tolerance-ESS offers a more precise and readily interpretable depiction of rank concern-
224
      ing approximation error. Unless a tolerance is specified, we use entropy-ESS throughout all our
225   experiments. An additional discussion comparing tolerance and entropy ESS, along with our code
226   for computing them, can be found in Section D.1.
227
228   Computational complexity of ESS. Since SVD scales cubically with the size of a square matrix,
229   the time complexity of computing ESS for a single layer with d channels, processing an input of
230   sequence length ℓ, is O((dℓ)3 ). Fortunately, most modern sequence models (outside of S5 (Smith
231   et al., 2023)) process the d channels independently (i.e. they are SISO). This means that each layer’s
232   dℓ × dℓ operator T can be decomposed into d independent ℓ × ℓ operators, which reduces the
233   ESS computation to an SVD of d independent operators (T̂ α := T αα ; α ∈ [d]). Consequently, a
234   per-channel ESS can be computed and summed to obtain the equivalent per-sequence index ESS.
235   This reduces the computational cost to O(dℓ3 ). Note that in models like attention, where channels
236   share the same recurrence within a single head, the time complexity is further reduced by a factor
237
      proportional to the head dimension. Additionally, in recurrent models with bounded TSS (n ≪ ℓ),
      a truncated SVD can be employed to decrease the time complexity to O(ℓ2 nd).
238
239
      Aggregation of ESS across model and data dimensions. Initially, we formulated ESS as a per-
240
      sequence index metric, which is general in that it applies to all models that realize an operator T .
241
      Recall in the last section, we extended ESS along the channel dimension for SISO models. For
242   LIV SISO models in particular, we can further extend ESS along the batch size dimension, since
243   ESS is a function of the input. This means that for a multi-layer LIV SISO model with m layers, d
244   channels, batch size b, and sequence length ℓ (which describes the entire set of models we analyze
245   in this work), ESS ∈ Rm×d×b×ℓ . Since ESS is a multidimensional tensor, there are various ways to
246   aggregate it across the model and data dimensions. We define two particular modes of aggregation
247   termed average ESS and total ESS as follows:
248                              1 X X X X
249            average ESS =                             ESSαβγ
                                                             i       total ESS = average ESS ∗ d
                               mdbℓ
250                                  α∈[d] β∈[m] γ∈[b] i∈[ℓ]
251
      Average ESS marginalizes across all of the ESS tensor dimensions by taking a mean, creating a per-
252   channel measure of ESS. Since average ESS is the metric used throughout most of our experiments,
253   we will refer to it as ESS unless otherwise specified. For models like softmax attention, where
254   average TSS (which is computed analogously to average ESS) depends only on the sequence index i
255   and thus remains constant as a function of model width (i.e. channel dimension), we instead capture
256   a model-dependent statistic, by first summing the ESS across channels and then averaging over the
257   remaining dimensions. This approach allows both ESS (and TSS) to vary as a function of model
258   width, a metric that we refer to as total ESS. For more details, refer to Sections D.1 and D.2.
259
260   4   E MPIRICAL VALIDATION OF E FFECTIVE S TATE -S IZE
261
262   To demonstrate the practical utility of ESS beyond its theoretical interpretation discussed in Section
263   3, we next turn to an empirical analysis. In this section, we examine ESS across a wide range of
264   tasks and models in order to understand how it varies across different regimes, with particular focus
265   placed on its relationship with model performance on memory-intensive tasks.
266
267   Task space. To explore ESS in an extensive, yet controlled, manner, we iterate on a set of syn-
268   thetic tasks proposed by Poli et al. (2024) which have been shown to effectively approximate model
269   performance on large-scale language tasks. Specifically, we train models on the multi-query asso-
      ciative recall (MQAR), selective copying, and compression tasks, each of which probes the ability

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

      task-model configurations from the sweep corresponding to each featurizer are shown.
279
280
      of models to effectively utilize their working memory. We note that here, we restrict the presentation
281
      of our results to MQAR and refer the reader to Section E.1 for the results on selective copying and
282
      compression, which showcase analogous trends.
283
284
      Model space. We explore four models within the scope of this analysis: gated linear attention
285
      (GLA), weighted linear attention (WLA), linear attention (LA) and softmax attention (SA). We
286
      choose this set of frameworks since, together, they capture a large portion of the space of modern
287   sequence models. The key distinctions between these models are as follows (more details can be
288   found in Section D.2):
289
290          • GLA layer: This layer implements the gated linear attention formulation described in Yang
291
               et al. (2024a), where the recurrent feature A (gating term) is input-varying, placing it in the
               same class as models like Liquid-S4 (Hasani et al., 2022) and Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2024;
292
               Dao & Gu, 2024).
293
294          • WLA layer: This layer is nearly identical to GLA, but with an input-invariant A matrix.
295            This lies in the same class as Hyena-S4D (Poli et al., 2023), RetNets (Sun et al., 2023), and
296            gated-convolutions in general.
297          • LA layer: This layer is based on Katharopoulos et al. (2020); A is not trainable and is
298            instead fixed as the identity matrix.
299
             • SA layer: This is the canonical attention layer which is similar to linear attention, but
300
               with the addition of a softmax non-linearity applied to the attention matrix (Vaswani et al.,
301            2023), enabling unbounded TSS.
302
303
304
      Experimental setup. In our analysis, we exhaustively sweep across the tasks and models (which
      are comprised of two sequence-mixing and two channel-mixing layers) detailed above.

      split our profiling of ESS into two sections: cross task-model analysis

      4.1   C ROSS TASK -M ODEL A NALYSIS
317
318   Our first goal is to understand how ESS empirically captures memory utilization by studying its
319   correlation with post-training MQAR performance across the entire task-model space. To appropri-
320   ately analyze ESS across tasks, we normalize it by the memory demands of MQAR, constructing an
321   adjusted form of ESS given by ESS/kv.
322
      Finding 1: Measured over the entire task-model space, ESS/kv exhibits a significantly higher
323   correlation with accuracy than TSS/kv (Figures 2, 3, 9a, 9b).


                                                                                  6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


324
      Note that the strong correlation between ESS/kv and accuracy highlights the efficacy of ESS as a
325   proxy for memory utilization. Furthermore, this finding underscores a significant gap in the ex-
326   planatory power between ESS and TSS, emphasizing the importance of analyzing models beyond
327   just their memory capacity.

337                                kv                                                          kv                                                           kv                                                                         kv
338
      Figure 4: Correlation between ESS and accuracy over the course of model training bucketed by TSS
339   and kv.
340
341
      Next, to further establish ESS as a proxy for memory utilization, we study how ESS evolves as
      a function of MQAR performance in a regime where TSS is kept fixed and, therefore, does not
342
      correlate with accuracy. We do this by analyzing ESS-accuracy correlation on a per-model, per-
343
      task basis over the course of training, uncovering several insights that serve as the basis for our
344   subsequent analysis.
345
346
       Finding 2: For less memory-intensive tasks trained using models with high TSS, we observe
       a lower correlation between ESS and performance compared to more memory-intensive tasks
347
       trained using a lower TSS (Figure 4).
348
349   This is in line with the interpretation of ESS as a measure of memory utilization. For easier tasks
350   that are learned by a model with high memory capacity, the model is not incentivized to increase its
351
      memory utilization beyond where it resides at initialization. In contrast, for difficult tasks that oper-
      ate in a memory-constrained regime, the model is forced to increase its memory utilization in order
352
      to learn, resulting in strong positive correlations between accuracy and ESS over training. 4 Digging
353
      a bit deeper, we find that this form of ESS analysis reveals two failure modes of model learning in
354   recurrent frameworks (which recall have bounded TSS): state saturation and state collapse.
355
356   State saturation refers to the scenario in which a model
                                                                                                                                                                                        GLA (TSS=8)                                    LA (TSS=8)                   WLA (TSS=8)

357
      has insufficient TSS to fully learn a task, resulting in its                                                                                                                      GLA (TSS=128)                                  LA (TSS=128)                 WLA (TSS=128)

                                                                                          1.0
      ESS converging near its TSS. This is reflected in its ES-        1.0
358
      S/TSS (which we refer to as state utilization) residing near
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        ESS/TSS
                                                                                                                                                                  accuracy


359
      1. We observe this in Figure 5 where we note that mod-           0.5                0.5
360   els with a TSS of 8 perform worse as the task difficulty
361   scales due to a saturated state. State collapse, on the other    0.0                0.0
                                                                                                                                                                             3             4                   5           6       7                3     4                             5   6   7
                                                                           2 2  2  2  2       2 2   2   2   2
362   hand, refers to the scenario in which a model has sufficient              kv                  kv
363   TSS to learn (or partially learn) a task, but its ESS fails to Figure 5: Accuracy and state utilization
364   increase during training, resulting in a heavily underuti- as a function of kv for low and high
365   lized state. With respect to state collapse, we observe the TSS models.
366   following:
367    Finding 3: For GLA and WLA, state collapse occurs in the high kv bucket of task-model space
368    (i.e. kv = 27 ) whereas for LA it does not (Figure 5). More generally, we find that LA has higher
369    state utilization than GLA and WLA.
370   While state saturation can only be solved by increasing TSS, state collapse can in principle be solved
371   by increasing ESS. Unlike TSS, which is a fixed hyperparameter of the model, one can modulate ESS
372   by changing various aspects of the model pipeline. Furthermore, even outside of the state collapse
373   regime, given the positive correlation between ESS and performance across the task-model space,
374   increasing ESS is a generally viable approach to improving model performance without sacrificing
375   efficiency. We explore this idea in the results to follow.
376

      The empty spots in the SA grid correspond to MQAR task constraints discussed in Section D.3.

389
390   5       A PPLICATIONS OF E FFECTIVE S TATE -S IZE
391
392
      In Section 4, we showed that changes in ESS are correlated with changes in performance, both
393
      across models and during model training, indicating its importance beyond just interpretability. In
394
      this section, we aim to push this insight further by understanding how we can leverage ESS to
395   both improve upon and improve our understanding of the existing performance-efficiency frontier
396   in sequence models. We partition our results based on the stage of model training at which we
397   apply ESS analysis: initialization-phase (Section 5.1), post-training (Section 5.2), and mid-training
398   (Section E.3). Finally, we move beyond synthetics and extend ESS to language in Section 5.3.
399
400
      5.1          I NITIALIZATION -P HASE A NALYSIS
401
402
      Initialization in weight space plays a crucial role in machine learning, significantly impacting model
403   convergence and training stability (Glorot & Bengio, 2010). We extend this concept to the ini-
404   tialization of recurrent models in state space, leaning on the intuition from Figure 2 that suggests
405   higher ESS can enhance performance. Namely, we illustrate how ESS at initialization can be used
406   to inform featurizer selection – the selection of the function that maps the input to the operator
407   T = f (u) or equivalently the recurrent features (Ai (u:i ), Bi (u:i ), Ci (u:i ), Di (u:i ))i∈[ℓ] – and ini-
408   tialization schemes. In doing so, we uncover design flaws of a prominent model, S6 (Mamba) (Gu
409   & Dao, 2024).
410
411   ESS-informed featurizer selection. To study the relationship between memory capacity and
412   memory utilization in S6, we remove the short convolutional layer in the Mamba block and stack
413   two of these modified blocks between SwiGLUs (Shazeer, 2020). Under the default MQAR task
414   settings outlined in Poli et al. (2024) (see Tables 2, 3, and 4 for details), we observe that S6 is en-
415   tirely unable to learn MQAR (accuracy ≈ 0) across multiple scales of TSS (16 - 256) as shown in
416   Figure 25. This aligns with results from Yang et al. (2024b), which independently demonstrate the
417
      poor performance of S6 without the additional short convolutional layer on a different in-context
      recall task. To investigate the cause, we look into how S6 is preconditioned to utilize its memory by
418
      computing its ESS when processing a Gaussian noise input, prior to training.
419
420    Finding 4: Figure 6a demonstrates that the ESS of S6 layers at initialization scales poorly with
421    respect to TSS, notably failing to increase monotonically. In contrast, GLA layers (Yang et al.,
422
       2024a), configured with hyperparameters to match the TSS, model width, number of layers, and
       hidden-state normalization of the S6 model (see Section D.2 and Table 2), exhibit greater and
423
       monotonically increasing ESS-TSS scaling at initialization (Figure 6a). Despite the architectural
424
       similarities between the S6 and GLA layers, Figure 6b demonstrates that, unlike S6, GLA achieves
425    accuracy improvements that correlate with increases in both TSS and ESS.
426
427
      Based on these findings, we conjecture that the poor ESS-TSS scaling of S6 prevents the model from
      effectively utilizing all of its states, irrespective of increases in memory capacity.
428
429
430   ESS-informed initialization scheme. To further investigate the differences between the afore-
431   mentioned S6 model and GLA model, we construct a composite model termed GLA-S6. This
      model adopts the feature-sharing structure of GLA (dividing dimensions into heads and sharing


                                                                                              8
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


432
      computations within a head), but applies the S6 featurization to the A matrix as follows:
433
434         GLA (original):       A = diag(sigmoid(W u)1/β )                                                  (4)
435                                                                              T
            GLA-S6:               A = diag(exp(−([1/α          2/α   ...   n/α] ⊙ softplus(W u)))).           (5)
436
437   Like S6, GLA-S6 fails to learn MQAR across the same range of TSS (see Figure 25) and exhibits
438   poor initialization-ESS scaling as shown in Figure 6a. Upon further inspection, we identify the
439   cause of poor ESS scaling: with each new state introduced, the arange term ([1 2 . . . n])
440   exponentially pushes new entries of A towards zero, negating the effects of additional states despite
441
      the increase in TSS. Therefore, to ameliorate the poor ESS scaling, we propose a simple solution:
      increase the normalization factor.
442
443    Finding 5: By scaling the normalization factor (α), Figure 6c shows that GLA-S6 achieves im-
444    provements in MQAR accuracy post-training, reflecting the impact of increasing its initialization-
445    ESS, despite the models having identical memory capacities.
446   These experiments demonstrate the efficacy of analyzing ESS at initialization, as they reveal how
447   different models are preconditioned to utilize their working memory. This analysis helps identify
448   potentially weak featurization and initialization schemes, enabling us to pinpoint shortcomings in
449   the S6 featurizer and implement a straightforward fix.
450
451   5.2    P OST-T RAINING A NALYSIS
452
453   Recall from Section 4.1 that we observed a strong correlation between ESS and post-training per-
454
      formance. Building on this insight, a natural question arises: can ESS be used for more than just
      performance analysis in the post-training setting? In this section, we answer this question by explor-
455
      ing an additional post-training application: model-order reduction.
456
457
      ESS-informed model-order reduction. Model-order reduction refers to the process of improv-
458
      ing model efficiency by reducing state-size while retaining performance. Previous works, such
459   as Massaroli et al. (2023), have explored the distillation of linear time-invariant (LTI) operators
460   (Tij = Ti+k,j+k ) into linear recurrences with small state-sizes using backpropagation. Other tech-
461   niques for model-order reduction such as modal truncation and balanced truncation (Beliczynski
462   et al., 1992; Gawronski & Juang, 1990) are also applicable to LTIs. In this study, however, we
463   are concerned with improving the efficiency of general LIVs. Since ESS serves as a lower bound
464   for the minimally realizable TSS (Section 3), we postulate that ESS can be used as a heuristic for
465   conducting model-order reduction.
466   To test this, we distill multiple GLA models (with TSS = 256) across various task regimes to
467   understand how the ESS of the original model (i.e. the teacher model) influences its ability to be
468   distilled into a smaller student model. We apply the technique outlined in Bick et al. (2024), where
469   the process can be divided into two steps. 1) matching the operators (min(||T(s) − T(t) ||2F /||T(t) ||2F ))
470   and 2) matching the output activations (min(||y(s) − y(t) ||22 /||y(t) ||22 )). More details can be found in
471   Section D.6. Figure 6d (and more comprehensively Figure 30) shows the relationship between the
472   ESS of the teacher model and the final activation loss during distillation.
473    Finding 6: Higher teacher ESS correlates with greater activation loss. The downstream perfor-
474    mance after single-layer distillation depends on both the teacher model’s average ESS and student
475    model’s TSS, with higher teacher ESS and lower student TSS resulting in greater performance
476    loss (Figure 29).
477   These findings position ESS as a useful heuristic for predicting model compressibility, enabling
478   efficient estimation of the potential for state-size reduction without extensive experimentation.
479
480
      5.3    S TATE M ODULATION OF L ARGE L ANGUAGE M ODELS
481
482   In contrast to synthetic tasks like MQAR, selective copying, and compression, we find that strong
483   recall performance on language depends not only on a model having sufficient ESS, but also on its
484   ability to dynamically modulate its ESS in response to inputs. We demonstrate that this explains why
485   linear attention, though effective on synthetic experiments (Section 4), is widely known to perform
      poorly on more complex language tasks (Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Arora et al., 2024).

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

      Figure 7: (a) The effect of separator tokens over Falcon Mamba 7B. See Section E.5 for plots
495
      of other open-weight models. (b) Comparison of standard perplexity and bigram recall perplexity
496   (Arora et al., 2023).
497
498   We begin by evaluating the (total ESS)i of open-weight pre-trained models. Borrowing notation
499   from Section 3.1, (total ESS)i is defined as follows:
500
                                                    1 X X X
501                               (total ESS)i =                   ESSαβγ
502
                                                   mb
                                                                                                   α∈[d] β∈[m] γ∈[b]
503
      Since we do not marginalize along the sequence dimension, we will examine this measure quali-
504
      tatively by observing how it changes as a function of the sequence index. Our analysis shown in
505   Figure 7a (and more broadly in Section E.5) reveals an intriguing phenomenon: ESS undergoes
506   a noticeable dip whenever an end-of-speech (EOS) token is encountered (refer to Section D.8 for
507   experimental details). This behavior aligns with our intuition regarding the role of EOS tokens and
508   provides a quantitative measure of how effectively a model can ‘reset’ or ‘forget’ past contexts when
509   transitioning between distinct segments of text. To investigate these effects in a more controlled en-
510   vironment, we trained four 1B parameter models (LA, WLA, GLA, and SA as described in Section
511   4) under identical conditions (see Table 9).
512    Finding 7: We observe a clear hierarchy in the degree of state modulation, which can be summa-
513    rized as follows: SA > GLA > WLA > LA (Figure 40).
514
      SA exhibits the most pronounced state modulation, beginning at a tolerance level of 1e−2, while also
515   realizing the largest ESS. GLA follows, with modulation emerging at a tolerance of 1e−1. WLA
516   shows minimal modulation, only detectable at a tolerance of 1.0, while LA displays no discernible
517   state modulation in response to separator tokens, demonstrating a clear lack of ability to modulate
518   ESS. The importance of state modulation becomes apparent when examining model performance.
519   Figure 7b illustrates that although standard perplexities (computed over a subset of the FineWeb
520   dataset (Penedo et al., 2024)) are similar across SA, WLA, and GLA, significant differences emerge
521   when considering the bigram recall perplexity metric introduced by Arora et al. (2023).
522    Finding 8: The ability of a model to recall information, as measured by bigram recall perplex-
523    ity across a pre-training dataset (rather than within a narrow task space), reveals a performance
524    hierarchy that closely mirrors the observed state modulation capabilities.
525   This finding suggests that state modulation serves as a key mechanism enabling models to effectively
526   manage complex context dependencies, directly impacting their performance on recall-heavy tasks.
527
528
529
      6                     C ONCLUSION
530
531
      In this work, we propose effective state-size (ESS), a measure of memory utilization in sequence
      models derived using dynamical systems theory. We motivate this metric as a valuable tool for ana-
532
      lyzing memory utilization in LIVs by demonstrating its strong correlation with performance across
533
      a wide range of synthetic tasks. In doing so, we find that ESS offers a versatile framework for under-
534   standing both the performance and efficiency of causal sequence models. Leveraging these insights,
535   we are able to construct novel, ESS-informed initializers, regularizers, and distillation strategies that
536   improve beyond the existing performance-efficiency trade-offs found in recurrent models. Finally,
537   we extend the ESS framework to language tasks, introducing the idea of state modulation – a con-
538   cept that proves crucial for performance on bigram recall tasks. Overall, this work establishes ESS
539   as a foundational tool for understanding and improving sequence model performance, opening new
      avenues for optimizing memory utilization and, more generally, model efficiency.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

      To ensure reproducibility, we utilized open-source models and tasks, adhering to default task con-
543   figurations unless otherwise specified. All crucial configurations are detailed in either the main text
544   or the appendix. Additionally, our code for computing both the tolerance-ESS and entropy-ESS is
545   provided in the appendix (Section D.1.3).

985        Figure 8: (a) ESS/kv and ∥ i Ai ∥F as a function of sequence length. (b) Accuracy of models as a

987
988
           To motivate the idea of increasing ESS mid-training, we revisit the concept of state collapse – a
           phenomenon that arises due to trainability issues in recurrent models (Figure 27), as discussed in
989
           Section 4.2. Recall that state collapse describes a failure mode of learning in GLA and WLA which,
990
           unlike LA, have learnable Ai matrices (where i denotes the index along the sequence dimension). To
991        see why this contributes to state collapse, we note that the values of the operator submatrices Hi are
992        disproportionately influenced by Ai , due to the presence of terms in the form of Ai−1 . . . A1 for each
993        i. Hence, the closer Ai lies to the 0-matrix, the faster these terms decay, reducing the numerical rank
994        of Hi . We demonstrate
                          Q          this empirically in Figure 8a, which shows that for both GLA and WLA,
995        ESS/kv and ∥ i Ai ∥F decrease as a function of sequence length. In contrast, for LA, whose A
996        matrix is given by the identity, ESS/kv remains large as the sequence length grows.
997        Given this insight, one approach to addressing state collapse in GLA and WLA is pushing the A
998        matrices towards the identity by adding the following term to the loss function: λ∥A − I∥F , where
999        λ denotes the strength of the regularizer and I denotes the identity. In doing so, we are effectively
1000       decaying the model towards LA, increasing its ESS and giving us the following:
1001
            Finding 9: GLA and WLA trained using the ESS-based regularization scheme described above
1002        outperform LA. When trained without it, they perform worse than LA (Figure 8b).
1003
1004
           For more commentary on this result, please refer to Section E.3.
1005
1006
           B      E XTENDED R ELATED W ORK
1007
1008
1009       Causal sequence models. From classical linear recurrences to modern sequence models like
1010       Transformers, a vast array of causal model architectures have emerged (Vaswani et al., 2023; Tsai
1011
           et al., 2019; Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Poli et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024a; Gu & Dao, 2024; Dao &
           Gu, 2024; Sun et al., 2023). In recent years, the ability to process sequences in parallel has become
1012
           increasingly critical, largely due to advancements in hardware accelerators such as GPUs. This need
1013
           for parallelism likely explains the growing popularity of models like attention, Mamba, and S4.
1014
1015       We observe that all of these models, which support parallelization across the sequence dimension,
1016       can be formulated using a linear system representation (y = T u) as detailed in the introductory and
1017
           theoretical sections (Sections 1 and 3). For this work, we categorize these models into two types:
           linear systems and systems with input-varying linear operators. The key distinction between these
1018
           two frameworks is that in the former, the operator T in input-invariant models is composed of fixed
1019
           system parameters, whereas in the latter, the parameters are dynamically generated from the input.
1020       Linear systems encompass both linear time-varying (LTV) and linear time-invariant (LTI) systems.
1021       Although LTV systems have been relatively unexplored in deep learning, several LTI models have
1022       been studied (Gu et al., 2022a;b; Smith et al., 2023; Orvieto et al., 2023; Parnichkun et al., 2024).
1023       Convolutional models use kernels h to construct Hankel matrices H, whose rank corresponds to the
1024       minimal state-size of the model (DeWilde & van der Veen, 1998). Massaroli et al. (2023) explored
1025       methods to reduce the order of models by leveraging the Hankel matrix. Notably, the submatrix Hi
           (defined in Theory 3.2) exhibits a Hankel structure in LTI models and provides per-sequence-index


                                                                                            19
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1026
       information. In this work, however, we do not explore Hankel matrices further, as they are not easily
1027   generalizable to LTV systems.
1028
1029   In contrast, systems with input-varying linear operators, which we abbreviate as LIVs, are char-
1030
       acterized by an operator T that is dynamically constructed through a featurizer and is defined by
       T = f (u). Examples of such models include softmax attention (Vaswani et al., 2023), linear atten-
1031
       tion (Katharopoulos et al., 2020), Liquid-S4 (Hasani et al., 2022), Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2024; Dao &
1032
       Gu, 2024), and gated linear attention (Yang et al., 2024a). Although these models may appear non-
1033   linear, they can still be represented like a linear system, enabling the application of linear analysis
1034   techniques. This forms the basis for the effective state-size metric.
1035
1036
       Interpretability. Analysis tools for sequence models can be categorized into two types: extrinsic
1037
       and intrinsic. Extrinsic tools focus solely on the input and output, treating the model’s internal
1038   processes as black boxes. This approach is highly generalizable, as it can be applied to any model,
1039   including those with non-linear recurrences. A notable example by Shen (2019) uses statistical
1040   measures such as mutual information to compute metrics that capture model “expressivity”. While
1041   these methods are versatile and applicable to various datasets, their generality makes them less
1042   effective at capturing the inner workings of causal sequence models, which is the primary focus of
1043   this work.
1044   Intrinsic tools, conversely, directly visualize the model’s internal mechanisms. A recently popular
1045   framework known as mechanistic interpretability provides one such example (Power et al., 2022).
1046   Mechanistic interpretability involves dissecting complex models to understand how specific com-
1047   ponents contribute to the model’s overall behavior (Cammarata et al., 2020). Unlike our work,
1048   mechanistic interpretability does not target the operator view of the model but instead emphasizes
1049   the functional roles and interactions of individual model components.
1050   For our purposes, we are primarily concerned with the visualization and analysis of classical and
1051   modern causal sequence models through the unifying lens of linear operators. Most analyses of
1052   these operators rely on visualization techniques (Olsson et al., 2022a; Vig, 2019; Abnar & Zuidema,
1053   2020; Ali et al., 2024; Xiao et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2024) to gain insights into the model’s internal
1054   processes. Visualizing the operator T is advantageous, as it reveals important features like the
1055   formation of induction heads, strong activations, diagonal and block-diagonal patterns, and Toeplitz
1056   structures. However, raw visualizations are largely qualitative and oftentimes do not provide the
1057   quantitative metrics necessary for effectively evaluating a model’s internal mechanisms – a gap we
1058
       aim to address in this work.
1059   Other, more quantitative, intrinsic methods perform some form of spectral analysis on the full oper-
1060   ator (Dong et al., 2023; Min & Li, 2024; Tumma et al., 2023; Bhojanapalli et al., 2020). A limitation
1061   of these approaches is that they often disregard the causal masking of T , which significantly impacts
1062   the model’s rank and singular values (Wu et al., 2024). As a result, the rank of the causal operator
1063   T alone lacks a clear interpretation.
1064   The proposed effective state-size metric is a method applicable to both linear systems and LIVs.
1065   As a quantitative proxy for memory utilization, it offers insights into the inner workings of causal
1066   sequence models, ensuring generality, usability, and interpretability.
1067
1068   Synthetic and language benchmarks. In this work, we build on synthetic tasks from the mech-
1069   anistic architecture design (MAD) framework introduced in (Poli et al., 2024). MAD defines a set
1070   of small-scale tasks designed to evaluate key model capabilities, such as in-context recall (Akyürek
1071   et al., 2024; Bhattamishra et al., 2023; Elhage et al., 2021; Olsson et al., 2022b). Training models
1072   on these tasks is efficient, making them well-suited for exploring a large space of tasks and models,
1073   as demonstrated in several prior works (Dupont et al., 2019; Arora et al., 2024; Fu et al., 2023). In
1074   this work, we investigate the effective state-size across a subset of the MAD tasks: multi-query as-
1075   sociative recall (MQAR), selective copying, and compression, varying the difficulty of each to gain
1076
       a nuanced understanding of how effective state-size evolves across these task landscapes.
1077   Among the synthetic tasks we examine, MQAR stands out in particular. Proposed by Arora et al.
1078   (2023), MQAR was designed to bridge the gap between synthetic and real language tasks explained
1079   by associative recall – the ability of a model to retrieve information based on relationships between
       different elements in its memory. This capability has long been sought after in the construction


                                                         20
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1080
       of sequence model architectures (Ramsauer et al., 2021; Ba et al., 2016); as such, we evaluate the
1081   performance of our models on MQAR to measure the benefits of using effective state-size to iterate
1082   on canonical frameworks used in sequence modeling.
1083
1084
       One notable aspect of MQAR observed in Arora et al. (2023) is that the size of the model cache
       needs to scale with the difficulty of the task to maintain performance. While this observation holds,
1085
       our work demonstrates that model cache size is an imperfect measure in this context due to the dis-
1086
       crepancy between memory capacity, as measured by theoretically realizable state size, and memory
1087   utilization, as measured by effective state-size. At a higher level, this demonstrates how our work
1088   provides a new perspective on analyzing memory-intensive synthetic tasks.
1089
1090
       While the MAD framework and synthetic tasks have shown correlations with model performance
       on large-scale language tasks, language itself poses a unique challenge. Models are tasked with
1091
       predicting the next token given previous tokens – a simple yet general objective. New tasks can be
1092
       created simply by altering the prompts, thereby expanding the range of possible task domains.
1093
1094   Although numerous language evaluation tasks – such as those in Hendrycks et al. (2021); Wang
1095   et al. (2024); Zellers et al. (2019) – have been proposed, they often probe a narrow task space and
1096
       tend to be brittle. For example, shuffling the order of multiple choices in MMLU can drastically
       change model rankings Alzahrani et al. (2024).
1097
1098   Unlike narrow benchmarks, perplexity scores can be computed across an entire pre-training dataset,
1099   covering a much broader task domain. However, small perplexity gaps between models make it a
1100   challenging metric for evaluation. Recently, Arora et al. found that much of the difference in per-
1101   plexity between models can be attributed to bigram perplexity – a measure of a model’s ability to
1102
       utilize the context and predict a successor token (second token of a bigram) given a repeated context
       token (first token of a bigram) within a sequence. They demonstrate that most of the average per-
1103
       plexity difference between a gated convolution model and an attention model stems from differences
1104
       in bigram perplexity, suggesting that recall is a key capability for language models.
1105
1106   The effective state-size analysis presented in this work reveals that strong recall performance as
1107   measured by bigram perplexity in language modeling tasks depends not only on memory capacity,
1108
       but also on a model’s ability to modulate its state-size within a given context.
1109
1110
1111
1112
1113
1114
1115
1116
1117
1118
1119

1121
1122
1123
1124

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1134
       C     T HEORETICAL BACKGROUND
1135
1136
1137   C.1     N OTATION
1138
1139   We adopt the following notation in this paper:
1140
1141           • Inputs, outputs, and operators follow flattened notation. i.e., u, y ∈ Rℓd and T ∈ Rℓd×ℓd .
1142             In particular, the original inputs and outputs with shape ℓ × d are flattened in row-major
1143             ordering, resulting in T having ℓ × ℓ sub-blocks, each of which is of size d × d.
1144
1145           • Tensor subscripts index sequence indices (time-step) and superscripts index channel/hidden
1146             dimensions. I.e., for an input u ∈ Rℓd , ui ∈ Rd denotes the input vector at sequence-index
1147             i, and uα ∈ Rℓ denotes the input vector for channel α. Similarly, Tij ∈ Rd×d denotes the
1148             linear weighing of uj on to yi .
1149
1150           • Indices within square brackets indicate matrix indices void of semantics (sequence index,
1151             channels, etc.). I.e., Ai[α,β] indexes row α and column β of matrix Ai .
1152
1153           • Semicolons within subscripts denote a product over ranges (A1;3 = A1 A2 A3 ).
1154
1155           • Tensor slices are denoted with colons and are inclusive over the ranges. I.e., u0:2 =
1156             u0 u1 u2 .
1157
1158
1159
       C.2     D ERIVATIONS AND P ROOFS

1209    Besides unveiling the relationship between the rank of the realized operator and the original state-
1210    size ni , the following insights can be drawn from the decomposition:
1211           • The causal portion Ci is the input-state projection matrix at time-step i (i.e., si = Ci u:i−1 )
1212             corresponding to Equation (C.2.1).
1213           • ESS (rank(Hi )) is simply the minimum rank between the causal and anti-causal projec-
1214             tions.
1215           • In conjunction with Theorem 3.2, we observe that the causally determinable minimal
1216             state-size (causal ESS) is equivalent to the rank of the causal projection. This insight
1217             allows us to construct a more efficient realization of the recurrence:
1218
                    – We can minimally factorize the causal projection as Ci = Li Ri , where Li ∈ Rni ×r
1219                  and Ri ∈ Rr×di , with r = rank(Ci ).
1220
                    – The right factor Ri becomes the new input-state projection matrix for Hi , effectively
1221                  reducing the state dimension to the causal ESS.
1222
                    – A∗i−1 and Bi−1
                                   ∗
                                       can be determined from Ri using the process outlined in Theorem
1223                             ∗
                      3.1, and Ci = Ci Li .

       C.2.3    T HE T RIVIAL R ECURRENCE R EALIZATION
1227
1228
       Any input-varying and input-invariant causal operator can be trivially realized with the following
1229
       recurrence:
1230
1231
1232
1233                                                       I(di)        0(di)
                                                   si+1 =          s +          u,
1234                                                        0(d) i       I(d) i                                               (C.2.5)
1235                                       yi = [Ti,0    Ti,1    ···   Ti,i−1 ] si + Ti,i ui .
1236
1237
1238   In simple terms, the state si stores each input from t ∈ [i − 1], which is then mapped to the output
1239   with operator features at row i. Note that in the case where the operator is input varying, the trivial
1240   realization upholds the causality of the featurization process (i.e. the features (Ai , Bi , Ci , Di )i∈[ℓ] of
1241   the trivial realization are causally determined). Moreover, the causally determined ESS (see Section
       C.2.2) for the trivially realized recurrence is equivalent to its TSS, as Ci = Idi .


                                                                  23
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1242
       C.2.4   E XISTENCE OF I NFINITE R ECURRENT R EALIZATIONS (P ROOF OF T HEOREM 3.1)
1243
1244   Theorem 3.1 Given any causal input-invariant operator T , there exist infinite variations of linear
1245   recurrences in the form of Equation (1) that realize an equivalent input-output operator.
1246
1247
1248   Proof. We first categorize the operator into two portions: the memoryless portion, where i = j, and
       the dynamical portion, where i > j. The memoryless portion can be trivially realized by setting
1249
       Di = Tii . For the dynamical portion, we draw inspiration from (DeWilde & van der Veen, 1998,
1250
       ch. 3) and approach the proof of existence by ansatz. The following steps outline the proof:
1251
1252
            1. Section C.2.2 demonstrates that, given a linear recurrence in the form of Equation (1), the
1253
               operator submatrix can be factorized into causal and anti-causal parts, where the causal
1254
               part represents the input-state projection matrix. Therefore, we proceed by making the
1255           following ansatz: for any operator submatrix Ti:,:i−1 ≡ Hi , Hi can be arbitrarily factorized
1256
               into Oi ∈ Rd(ℓ−i)×ni and Ci ∈ Rni ×di , and that Ci represents the input-state projection at
1257           time-step i (i.e., si = Ci u:i−1 ).
1258
1259        2. Construct the dynamic features (Ai , Bi , Ci )i∈[ℓ] such that the assumption above holds.
1260           Note that we additionally assume the initial and final states to be 0 without loss of general-
1261           ity, therefore the realization of C0 , A0 , Aℓ−1 , and Bℓ−1 could be ignored.
1262            (a) Set Ci = Oi[:d−1] to obtain (Ci )i∈[1,ℓ] , as given the assumptions above, the first set of
1263                rows of Oi linearly projects si onto yi − Di ui , which is identical to Ci in Equation
1264                (1).
1265            (b) Set Bi−1 = Ci[:,−d:] to obtain (Bi )i∈[ℓ−1] , for which the identity can be obtained by
1266                deconstructing the input-state projection matrix Ci and equating its assumed state si
1267                with Equation (1).
1268                                        si = Ai−1 si−1 + Bi−1 ui−1
1269                                                                                                   (C.2.6)
                                               = Ci[:,:−d−1] u:i−2 + Ci[:,−d:] ui−1 .
1270
1271            (c) Using the same state-dynamics equation, we could equate the assumed state-
1272                projection matrices with each other, obtaining (Ai )i∈[1,ℓ−1] :
1273
                                                       si+1 = Ai si + Bi ui
1274
                                                    Ci+1 u:i = Ai Ci u:i−1 + Ci+1[:,−d:] ui
1275                                                                                                     (C.2.7)
1276                                     Ci+1[:,:−d−1] u:i−1 = Ai Ci u:i−1
1277                                                     Ai = Ci+1[:,:−d−1] Ci+ .
1278
1279        3. Verify that the realized recurrence maps back to the original operator Tij , proving that
1280           arbitrary factorizations (of which there are infinite variations) of the operator submatrices
1281           can be used to construct equivalent operators.
1282                                                                        +                  +
                  Tij = Ci Ai−1 · · · Aj+1 Bj = Oi[:d−1] Ci[:,:−d−1] . . . Cj+2 Cj+2[:,:−d−1] Cj+1 Cj+1[:,−d:]
1283
1284
                                               = Oi[:d−1] Ci[:,:−d−1] I[:,:(j+1)d−1] I[:,−d:]
1285                                           = Oi[:d−1] Ci[:,jd:(j+1)d−1] = Hi[:d−1,jd:(j+1)d−1] = Tij .

1290   As an example, Hi can be factorized minimally with SVD as follows:
1291
                                                          1/2      1/2
1292                                     Oi Ci = (U(r) D(r) )(D(r) V(r) ),
1293
1294   where U(r) ∈ Rm×r , D(r) ∈ Rr×r , V(r) ∈ Rr×n are the r-truncated SVD decompositions, and
1295   r = rank of Hi ∈ Rm×n . Theorem 3.2 demonstrates that these factors realizes a recurrence with
       minimal state-size.


                                                         24
           Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1296
           C.3     M INIMAL R ECURRENT R EALIZATION (P ROOF OF T HEOREM 3.2)
1297
1298
            Theorem 3.2 The rank of the operator submatrix (Hi ≡ Ti:,:i−1 ) determines the minimal state
1299
            size required to represent the causal operation (y = T u) as a recurrence.
1300
1301       Proof. The proof of Theorem 3.1 shows that the operator submatrices Hi can be decomposed ar-
1302       bitrarily into two state-projection matrices, Oi and Ci , whose inner product dimension defines the
1303       state size of its recurrent realization at sequence index i. By the rank-nullity theorem, rank(Hi )
1304       represents the minimum inner product dimension of any such state-projection matrices and thus
1305       corresponds to the minimally realizable state size of the operator T at sequence index i.
1306
1307       D     M ETHODS
1308
1309       D.1     A DDITIONAL D ETAILS ON C OMPUTING ESS
1310
1311       D.1.1    C OMPUTING ESS FOR SISO MODELS
1312
           In this section, we provide additional details on the distinction between computing ESS in single-
1313
           input single-output (SISO) models like S4 and multi-input multi-output (MIMO) models like S5.
1314
1315       Recall in Sections 3 and C.1, we introduced flattened notation, as it offers a general framework for
1316       formulating a wide range of operators and recurrences. Namely, a MIMO layer like S5 (Smith et al.,
1317       2023), which mixes both the channels and sequence simultaneously, can be formulated as y = T u
1318
           (with the operator realization outlined in C.2.1) in the same way a SISO layer like S4 (Gu et al.,
           2022a) can, which only mixes the sequence. The difference between these two models lies in the
1319
           structure of T : for models that only mix the sequence, such as S4, Tij is diagonal; for S5, it is dense.
1320
1321       Note that since all of the models in our experiments are SISO, we can compute the ESS indepen-
1322       dently for each channel using the standard operator formulation T αα ∈ Rℓ×ℓ , where α ∈ [d]. This
1323       approach is significantly more efficient than computing ESS for the multi-channel (flattened) rep-
1324
           resentation. Furthermore, in the case of attention layers, the computation can be further reduced to
           only the h independent heads, as the operator (i.e. the attention matrix) is shared across channels
1325
           within the same head.
1326
1327
           D.1.2    T OLERANCE VS ENTROPY ESS
1328
1329       Regarding the distinction between the entropy and tolerance-based forms of ESS, we note that
1330       entropy-ESS is a valuable summary metric because its computation is independent of any specific
1331       tolerance value chosen. However, it can potentially be misleading when comparing ESS across se-
1332       quence indices due to the unequal normalization applied to the singular values. Conversely, when
1333       comparing entropy-ESS across different operators, it can be useful as the normalization removes
1334       the effect of the norm of the operator. Moreover, in contrast to the tolerance-based metric which is
1335
           discrete, entropy-ESS can assume continuous values ranging from 1 to |Σi |.
1336       In most of our experiments, we observe consistent trends between entropy-ESS and tolerance-ESS
1337       when the metrics are marginalized over the sequence length. Therefore, unless stated otherwise, our
1338       figures are presented using the entropy-ESS. In cases where we require ESS comparison across the
1339       sequence dimension, we instead plot ESS for multiple tolerance values. Finally, we note that for the
1340       analyses presented in Section 4, when computing ESS, we average across 8 samples (batch-size).
1341
           For the rest of the ESS analyses, we average across 32 samples.
1342
1343
           D.1.3    P Y T ORCH I MPLEMENTATION
1344       Below, we provide a PyTorch implementation of various ESS metrics and helper functions that were
1345       leveraged in our analyses:
1346
       1   import torch
1347   2
1348   3 def T2H_i(T, i, d=1):
1349   4     """
       5     Extract H_i from T.


                                                              25
            Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1350
        6
1351    7       Args:
1352    8           - T: Flattened operator with shape [..., d*L, d*L].
1353    9           - i: Index of H (H_i) to retrieve.
1354   10           - d: Block size for multi-channel flattened operator
                representation (default is 1).
1355
       11
1356   12        Returns:
1357   13            - H_i: Submatrix of the operator at index i.
1358   14        """
1359   15        return T[...,d*i:,:d*i]
       16
1360
       17 @torch.no_grad()
1361   18 def T2Ss(T, d=1):
1362   19     """
1363   20     Converts an operator into a list of singular values (Ss).
1364   21
       22       Args:
1365
       23           - T: Flattened operator with shape [..., d*L, d*L]
1366   24           - d: Block size for multi-channel flattened operator
1367            representation (default is 1).
1368   25

1369   26        Returns:
       27            - Ss: A list of singular values for each sequence index in T.
1370
       28        """
1371   29        seqlen = T.size(-2)//d
1372   30        Ss = []
1373   31        for i in range(1, seqlen):
1374   32            H_i = T2H_i(T, i, d)
       33            _, S_i, _ = torch.svd(H_i)
1375
       34            Ss.append(S_i)
1376   35        return Ss
1377   36
1378   37 @torch.no_grad()
1379   38 def Ss2ToleranceESS(Ss, tol=1e-4):
       39     """
1380
       40     Computes the tolerance-ESS from the list of singular values.
1381   41
1382   42        Args:
1383   43            - Ss: List of singular values.
1384   44            - tol: Tolerance value.
       45
1385
       46        Returns:
1386   47            - tolerance-ESS
1387   48        """
1388   49        ranks = []
1389   50        for SV in Ss:
       51            rank = torch.sum(SV>=tol, dim = -1)
1390
       52            ranks.append(rank)
1391   53        ranks = torch.stack(ranks, dim=-1)
1392   54        return ranks
1393   55

1394    @torch.no_grad()
       56
        def Ss2EntropyESS(Ss, clip=1e-12):
       57
1395
     58     """
1396 59     Computes the entropy-ESS from the list of singular values.
1397   60
1398   61       Args:
1399   62           - Ss: List of singular values.
       63           - clip: clips probabilities below this value avoiding numerical
1400
                instabilities when the probabilities are too numerically close to 0.
1401   64
1402   65        Returns:
1403   66            - entropy-ESS
       67        """


                                                         26
            Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1404
       68         ranks = []
1405   69         for SV in Ss:
1406   70             p = SV/SV.sum(dim=-1)[..., None]
1407   71             p = torch.clip(p, clip)
1408   72             H = -torch.sum(p * torch.log(p), dim=-1)
       73             rank = torch.exp(H)
1409
       74             ranks.append(rank)
1410   75         ranks = torch.stack(ranks, dim=-1)
1411   76         return ranks
1412
1413        Example usage (Python-pseudocode):
1414    1 >>> out = model(u, output_attentions=True)
1415    2 >>> # T shape: [bs, layers, heads, len, len]
        3 >>> T = out.attention_matrix
1416
        4 >>> Ss = T2Ss(T) # List of singular values
1417    5 >>> # ESS shape [bs, layers, heads, len-1]
1418    6 >>> ESS = Ss2ToleranceESS(Ss, tol=1e-3)
1419    7 >>> mean_ESS = torch.mean(ESS)

1420
1421        We note that calculating the effective rank may cause numerical instability when pm i approaches 0
1422
            due to the logarithmic term. This is partially mitigated by clipping the normalized singular values,
            as shown above.
1423
1424
1425
            D.2   F ORMULATION OF THE F EATURIZERS
1426        In this section, we first establish the equivalence between linear attention and state-space models,
1427        then proceed with formulating the LIVs discussed in this paper.
1428
1429        Linear attention and state-space model equivalence. We begin by demonstrating that linear
1430        attention models are state-space models, serving as the foundation for the subsequent formulation
1431        of featurizers for other models, such as gated linear attention and weighted linear attention.
1432
            A single linear attention head with dimension d/h, typically formulated as
1433
1434
                                                          y = qk T v,                                    (D.2.1)
                                 ℓ×d/h
1435        in which q, k, v ∈ R        are input features. They can be reformulated as recurrences with matrix-
1436        valued states si ∈ Rd/h×d/h as follows (Katharopoulos et al., 2020):
1437                                                   si = si−1 + ki viT
                                                                                                         (D.2.2)
1438                                                      yi = qiT si ,
1439
1440        Without loss of generality, applying column-major flattening to the matrix-valued state and treating
1441        vi as the input ui , the recurrence can be formulated like Equation (1), by setting Ai = I(d/h)2 and:
                      ki1
                                                       

1446                .                                              qi1    ···    qi      0     ···     0     ···   0     ···      0
                                 ..    ..          ..                                                  d/h
                                                                                          qi1
                    .
                                                                  0       ···      0           ···    qi     ···   0     ···      0 

                    0       d/h

                                                d/h
