                                                                                                                                 (D.2.3)
1454
1455
1456        Notice that each individual channel forms a single-input-single-output (SISO) recurrence (like many
1457        of the SSM architectures including S4, S6, Mamba2, and more [27; 26; 16; 48; 49]), as there is no
            mixing across channels. Additionally, each of these SISO recurrences has a state-size of d/h.


                                                                          27
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1458
       Formulation of the featurizers. For the sake of completeness, we additionally characterize the
1459   “values” feature in attention-like models, with fu (u) as follows:
1460
1461                                       si+1 = Ai si + Bi fu (ui )
                                                                                                    (D.2.4)
1462                                         yi = CiT si + Di fu (ui ).
1463
1464   The following lists the formulations of the recurrent featurizers based on the results above, along
1465   with their per-channel (i.e. average) TSS and total TSS:
1466          • Linear Attention (LA):
1467
1468                 Ak = I,     k
                                Bi−1 = RoPE(WBk ui ),        Cik = RoPE(WCk ui ),    fu (ui ) = Wuk ui ,
1469                                                                                                (D.2.5)
1470
                where WCk , WBk , Wuk ∈ Rd/h×d ; d and h represent the number of channels and heads,
1471            respectively. A is a fixed identity matrix. Per-channel TSS is nki = d/h, and the total TSS
1472
                is d2 /h. Each channel c ∈ [d] is grouped into heads, where the head index corresponding
                to the channel is given by k = ⌊ch/d⌋, and within a head, all corresponding projection
1473
                matrices (WCk , WBk , etc.) are weight tied (shared). Moreover, a rotational positional
1474
                encoding (RoPE) is by default applied to the B and C projections (Su et al., 2023).
1475
1476          • Gated Linear Attention (GLA):
1477
                                        Aki−1 = diag(sigmoid(WAk 2 WA1 ui )1/β ),
1478                                                                                                (D.2.6)
                                     k
1479                                Bi−1 = WBk ui ,     Cik = WCk ui ,        fu (ui ) = Wuk ui ,
1480
                where besides having projections identical to those in LA, WA1 ∈ R16×d and WAk 2 ∈
1481
1482            Rd/h×16 . Like LA, per-channel TSS is nki = d/h, and the total TSS is d2 /h. By default,
1483
                β is set to 16.
1484          • Weighted Linear Attention (WLA):
1485
1486                                          Ak = diag(sigmoid(Aˆk )1/β ),
                                                                                                    (D.2.7)
1487                                 k
                                    Bi−1 = WBk ui ,     Cik = WCk ui ,        fu (ui ) = Wuk ui ,
1488
1489            where WC , WB , and Wu are identical to those in LA, and Âk ∈ Rd/h is explicitly
1490            parameterized and initialized to 0. Like LA, per-channel TSS is nki = d/h, and the total
1491            TSS is d2 /h.
1492
              • Softmax Attention (SA):
1493
1494                                  B̂ik = RoPE(WBk ui ),     Ĉik = RoPE(WCk ui ),
1495                                                                                                (D.2.8)
                                     T k = softmax(Ĉ k (B̂ k )T ),       fu (ui ) = Wuk ui ,
1496
1497            where WC , WB , and Wu are identical to those in LA, and T can be converted into a
1498            recurrence using the trivial realization in Equation C.2.5. Therefore, the per-channel TSS
1499            is i, and total TSS is id. Like LA, a rotational positional encoding (RoPE) is by default
1500            applied to the B̂ and Ĉ projections (Su et al., 2023).
1501
              • S6 (Gu & Dao, 2024):
1502
1503                           ∆c = softplus(W∆
                                              c
                                                ui + bc ),      Aci−1 = diag(exp(−Â∆c )),
1504                                        c
                                                                                                    (D.2.9)
                                           Bi−1 = ∆c WB ui ,          Ci = WC ui ,
1505
                                                                      T
1506            where Â ∈ Rn is initialized to [1 2 · · · n] , c is the channel index, WC , WB ∈
1507            Rn×d , and W∆ c
                                ∈ R1×d . Here, per-channel TSS is n, and total TSS is nd. Note that by
1508            setting n = d/h, S6 resembles GLA, with the following exceptions:
1509                   – S6 has only one (not h) different projection matrices for B and C.
1510
                       – S6 has channel-wise projections for the input-varying discretization applied to
1511
                         B and C.


                                                        28
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1512
1513
                      – S6 has an explicitly parameterized vector valued Â.
1514                  – S6 has some minor differences in the non-linearity applied to keep 0 < A < 1.
1515                    Similar to GLA, S6 also has diagonal A matrices, whereas in Mamba2 Dao &
1516                    Gu (2024), the A matrix is scalar-valued. However, the B and C projections in
1517
                        Mamba2 more closely resemble that of GLA.
1518          • GLA-S6:
1519
1520              Ahi−1 = diag(exp(−[1/α        2/α   ...   n/α]T ⊙ softplus(WAh2 WA1 ui ))),
                               h
                                                                                                (D.2.10)
1521                          Bi−1 = WBh ui ,    Cih = WCh ui ,   fu (ui ) = Wuk ui ,
1522
                GLA-S6 is a combination of S6 and GLA such that the B and C projections are identical
1523
                to that of GLA, while A is featurized similarly to S6. Namely, it has identical WB , WC ,
1524
                and Wu to those found in LA which means that the per-channel TSS is d/h and total TSS
1525                                                                                      T
                is d2 /h. The A matrix is featurized with the arange term ([1 2 · · · n] ) like S6. We
1526
                additionally added a normalization hyperparameter α, which controls the rate at which
1527
                elements of A decay to 0.

       D.3   E MPIRICAL VALIDATION
1567
1568   Here, we provide details on the task-model sweep presented in Section 4. Table 1 lists the hyper-
1569   parameters that were exhaustively swept across to generate the task-model space. Note that the
1570   hyperparameter controlling the task difficulty is task-dependent (for more details, see Poli et al.
1571   (2024)).
1572   For the MQAR and selective copying tasks, a default vocab size of 8192 (Arora et al., 2023) was
1573   used for all models. For the compression tasks, the vocab size was varied to modulate task difficulty,
1574   as shown in Table 1. Any other task settings not specified here are defaulted to those presented in
1575   Arora et al. (2023). Two important constraints on the tasks from Arora et al. (2023) which we also
1576   utilize in our experiments are as follows: MQAR task requires that
1577
                                            4 ∗ num kv pairs ≤ seq len
1578
1579   and the selective copying task requires that
1580
1581
                                      2 ∗ num tokens to copy + 1 < seq len
1582   Any of the task configurations from Table 1 that violate these conditions were not trained. This is
1583   why the SA plot in Figure 4 has empty spots in the grid.
1584
       Finally, we note that all architectures analyzed here consist of 4 layers: 2 sequence mixing layers
1585
       (i.e. one of GLA, LA, WLA or SA) and 2 channel mixing layers (i.e. MLPs).
1586
1587     Configuration                                  Value(s)
1588
1589     Tasks                                          MQAR, selective copying, compression
1590     Num. key-value pairs                           8, 16, 32, 64, 128
1591     Num. tokens to copy                            8, 16, 32, 64, 128
1592     Vocab size (compression)                       8, 16, 32, 64, 128
1593     Vocab size (MQAR and selective copying)        8192
1594     Sequence length                                64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048
1595
         Model (featurizer)                             GLA, LA, WLA, SA
1596
         Model width                                    64, 128, 256, 512
1597
         Number of heads                                4, 8
1598
1599
         Optimizer                                      AdamW
1600     Learning Rate                                  0.002
1601     Weight Decay                                   0.1
1602     Batch Size                                     64
1603     Epochs                                         70
1604     Steps Per Epoch                                2000
1605     Num. Training Samples                          128k
1606
         Num. Testing Samples                           6.4k
1607
1608
1609
                            Table 1: Set of hyperparameters for the task-model sweep.
1610
1611
       Regarding the post-hoc analysis performed on the sweep, we note the following:
1612
1613                                                                                    model width
              • Since the average TSS computed over the channels (which equals number        of heads for GLA,
1614            LA, and WLA) explains more meaningful variation with respect to memory utilization than
1615            model width and number of heads individually, we consolidate those two dimensions into
1616            one by analyzing across the average TSS axis. For SA, since average TSS is a function
1617            of the task rather than model hyperparameters (see Equation C.2.5 and Section D.2), we
1618            instead compute the sum of TSS over all d channels, given by the total TSS per layer = d∗i.
1619            In any cases where the average/total qualifier is not specified, note that we are referring to
                the average ESS or TSS.


                                                         30
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1620
                • Since we analyze the recurrent models across the average TSS dimension, we compute
1621              average ESS in the plots presented in Section 4.1 in order to compare ESS and TSS as
1622              proxies for performance. Similarly, since we analyze the SA models across the total TSS
1623              dimension, we compute total ESS for those plots. However, we note that plots for both the
1624              average/total ESS and TSS are presented in Section E.1.
1625
                • When we marginalize across dimensions, we average across all models in that bucket of
1626
                  task-model space. For example, in Figure 4, for each (TSS, kv) pair, we average over the
1627              correlations of all models that correspond to that pair. Note, however, that we never average
1628              across tasks (i.e. MQAR, selective copying, compression) or featurizers (i.e. GLA, LA,
1629              WLA, SA).
1630
                • When we compute cross-model correlations (Figure 2) for SA, we filter out models which
1631
                  have an accuracy > 0.95. This is done in order to observe meaningful variation as a func-
1632
                  tion of (total ESS)/kv and (total TSS)/kv since many of the SA models obtain an accuracy
1633              of 1.
1634
1635            • When we compute within-model correlations (Figure 4) for MQAR, we drop epoch 0 from
1636
                  the computation since we observe a phase at the start of training in which ESS tends to
                  decrease, but accuracy does not change. We elaborate on this phenomenon in Section E.1
1637
                  and hope to characterize it further in future work.
1638
1639            • Regarding the task-adjusted forms of ESS and TSS which, in the case of MQAR, are com-
1640              puted by normalizing the raw ESS value by the number of kv-pairs in the task, we note
1641              that this normalization factor is critical for observing the cross task-model correlations pre-
1642
                  sented in Figure 2. In particular, in Figure 9, we find that correlations across the task-model
                  space break down when examining the unnormalized ESS. This points to the higher-level
1643
                  notion that ESS is expected to scale with the memory demands of the task.
1644
1645            • We interpret the state utilization of a model, which is given by ESS/TSS, as a proxy for what
1646              portion of the memory capacity of the network is realized in practice. By definition, state
1647
                  utilization takes on values ranging continuously from 0 to 1. Recall that a state utilization
                  near 1 is indicative of state saturation.
1648
1649            • While for most of the ESS analysis conducted on the sweep we use the entropy-ESS, we
1650              note that for the state utilization plot presented in Figure 5, we use the tolerance-ESS with
1651              a tolerance level set at 1e-3. We do this because we find that entropy-ESS fails to capture
1652              the state collapse phenomenon. This is because state collapse is primarily dictated by
1653
                  the magnitude of the singular values, as opposed to the relative decay rate of the entire
                  spectrum. In particular, if all of the singular values are close to 0, the layer is likely failing
1654
                  to learn an expressive state, resulting in poor performance. Due to the normalization applied
1655
                  to the spectrum, the entropy-ESS metric may potentially present this state as having a high
1656              effective rank; however, in practice, we know that this is a misrepresentation of the true
1657              dynamics. Tolerance-ESS, in contrast, appropriately captures the dynamics of the state
1658              with respect to the norm of the operator. Because of this, whenever we analyze ESS as it
1659              pertains to state collapse (e.g. Figure 8a), we present the tolerance-ESS instead.
1660
1661

       Regarding the regularization scheme itself, since we examine models with two sequence mixing
1754   layers, we explore the following strategies: regularizing both layers, only regularizing the first layer
1755   and only regularizing the second layer. Empirically, we find that only regularizing the second layer
1756   performs the best and is thus the result presented in Figure 8b. We elaborate on why this is the most
1757   successful strategy in Section E.3.

1760   D.6   ESS-I NFORMED M ODEL -O RDER R EDUCTION
1761
1762   The teacher models used in the distillation experiments are 2-layer GLA models (Yang et al., 2024a)
1763
       with dimension = 128 and TSS = 256 (num heads = 8 and expand k = 16). We checkpointed the
       models every 10 epochs while training on MQAR across different task difficulties. The task ranges
1764
       are given as follows:
1765
1766
              • Sequence length: [512, 1024, 2048]
1767
1768          • Number of Key-Value Pairs: [64, 128]
1769
1770   Other settings follow the defaults shown in Table 4. For each task difficulty pair, we repeated the
1771   training run with three different seeds. For each teacher model checkpoint, both layers were distilled
1772   independently with student models of different state-sizes (16, 32, 64, and 128). Distillation settings
1773   are shown in Table 6.
1774
       The ESS metric in Figures 6d, 30, and 31 was computed by taking the minimum across the batch
1775
       and channels, evaluated at the mid-point of the sequence (ℓ/2). Using the mid-point of the sequence
1776   as a summary statistic was done to reduce compute. The midpoint in particular was chosen as it is
1777   the point in the sequence at which Hi has the greatest dimensions, retaining the largest amount of
1778   information from the original operator. Note that in practice, where a large task space isn’t being
1779   tested to validate our approach, a more thorough computation of ESS across sequence length is
1780   feasible. Other marginalization approaches such as taking the maximum or average across the batch
1781   and channels also show similar trends, but we found taking the minimum to best demonstrate the
       trend.


                                                           33
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                                       Configuration                   Value
1783
1784                                   Optimizer                      AdamW
1785                                   Batch Size                        1
1786
                                       Learning Rate                   0.001
                                       Weight Decay                     0.0
1787
1788                                   Training Steps (Operator)        800
1789                                   Dropout (Operator)               0.2
1790                                   Training Steps (Activation)     3200
1791                                   Dropout (Activation)             0.2

       D.7   ESS A NALYSIS FOR H YBRID N ETWORKS
1798
1799
1800   In our ESS analysis applied to hybrid networks, we restrict our scope to GLA-SA hybrids. In
1801   particular, we explore the following two settings:
1802
1803
1804         • 8-layer hybrid networks in which 4 layers are sequence mixers (i.e. one of GLA or SA)
1805           and 4 layers are channel mixers (i.e. MLPs). We exhaust all possible hybrid networks (of
1806           which there are 16) and perform post-training, per-layer ESS analysis on the networks. We
1807           train these hybrid models on MQAR with task-model settings given below in Table 7.
1808
1809
1810         • 16-layer hybrid networks in which 8 layers are sequence mixers (i.e. one of GLA or SA)
1811           and 8 layers are channel mixers (i.e. MLPs). Here, we explore all combinations of hybrid
1812
               networks that follow the Jamba hybridization policy (Lieber et al., 2024) and perform post-
               training, per-layer ESS analysis on the networks. We train these hybrid models on MQAR
1813
               with task-model settings given below in Table 8.
1814
1815
1816
1817                                    Configuration                 Value
1818                                    Sequence length                2048
1819                                    Num. KV Pairs                   512
1820                                    KV Dist. Const.                 0.1
1821                                    Optimizer                    AdamW a

1860   D.8   S TATE M ODULATION OF L ARGE L ANGUAGE M ODELS
1861
1862   State modulation of open-weight models. The following randomly generated sentences were
1863   used to study the effects of separator tokens on state modulation in open-weights pre-trained lan-
1864   guage models.
1865   <bos>Mangoes are rich in vitamin C and can be blended into a refreshing smoothie<sep> Gi-
1866   raffes are the tallest mammals on Earth due to their long necks and legs<sep> She collects vintage
1867   typewriters from the 1940s<sep> Jupiter’s Great Red Spot is a giant storm that has been raging
1868   for hundreds of years<sep>
1869
1870
1871
1872   State modulation on custom-trained 1B models. For our custom-trained 1B language models,
1873   we used longer sentences, as state modulation patterns were less discernible with shorter sequences.
1874   A collection of randomly generated sentences is shown below:
1875   <bos>The deep blue ocean, teeming with an extraordinary array of marine life, from the smallest
1876   plankton to the largest whales, stretches out infinitely towards the horizon, a vast and mysterious
1877   expanse that has captivated the imaginations of explorers, scientists, and poets for centuries, hid-
1878   ing within its depths secrets yet to be discovered and stories yet to be told<sep> In a bustling city
1879   where skyscrapers tower over narrow streets filled with the constant hum of cars and the chatter of
1880   pedestrians, a small café, nestled between two imposing buildings, offers a quiet refuge for those
1881
       seeking a moment of peace, with the comforting aroma of freshly brewed coffee and the soft sound
       of jazz music playing in the background, creating a cozy ambiance that feels like a world away
1882
       from the urban chaos outside<sep> The ancient oak tree, with its gnarled branches stretching
1883
       wide and its thick, sturdy trunk standing firm against the passage of time, has witnessed genera-
1884   tions of families grow, seasons change, and countless stories unfold beneath its expansive canopy,
1885   becoming a silent guardian of the park, offering shade to those who seek solace and a sense of
1886   continuity in a rapidly changing world<sep>
1887
       We note that the specific sentences and their order are not crucial to this analysis. Similar patterns
1888
       have emerged with various sentence arrangements, provided the sentences are sufficiently long.
1889
       Training settings are outlined in Table 9.

       E                    E XTENDED E XPERIMENTAL R ESULTS
1945
1946
       E.1                   E MPIRICAL VALIDATION
1947
1948
       In this section, we provide additional results and commentary from the sweep detailed in Section D.3
1949
       that were not presented in the main portion of the paper. One thing to note is that most of the ESS
1950   results presented in Section 4 were computed using the entropy-ESS. However, we also computed
1951   ESS using the tolerance-based approach to affirm that both forms of ESS showcase similar trends.
1952   In particular, we examined tolerances of 1e-1, 1e-3 and 1e-5. Since we observe similar trends across
1953   tolerances, we provide plots for a tolerance of 1e-3 below and omit the others for the sake of brevity.
1954
1955   E.1.1                        S TATE C OLLAPSE C ONTINUED
1956
1957   Here, we continue our discussion on the state collapse phenomenon presented in Section 4.2. In
1958   particular, while we assert that state collapse is observable across all TSS in the high kv bucket for
1959   GLA/WLA, Figure 5 shows that accuracy differences between LA and GLA/WLA are only evident
1960   in the high TSS/high kv bucket of the task-model space. This is because state saturation is acting as a
1961   confounder, worsening performance in LA (see Figure 5 when TSS is 8). Therefore, although state
1962
       collapse in GLA/WLA does not result in worse performance than LA in this specific task-model
       setting, it remains an issue even for models with smaller states when trained on sufficiently difficult
1963
       tasks. This is the motivation behind the task-model setting explored in Section A.
1964
1965
1966   E.1.2                        E NTROPY-ESS MQAR R ESULTS C ONTINUED
1967

1988   Figure 9: (a) TSS/kv vs accuracy across featurizers. This demonstrates that TSS/kv (i.e. memory
1989   capacity) is a worse proxy for model performance than ESS/kv as discussed in Section 4. (b) (total
1990   TSS)/kv vs accuracy across featurizers. This demonstrates that (total TSS)/kv is a worse proxy for
1991   model performance than (total ESS)/kv. (c) ESS/kv vs accuracy across featurizers. (d) (total ESS)/kv
1992   vs accuracy across featurizers. (e) ESS/TSS (i.e. state utilization) vs accuracy across featurizers. We
1993   note that models that saturate their state tend to perform worse on the task, which is evidence of the
1994   state saturation phenomenon discussed in Section 4.2. The models that do not saturate their state
1995   but still perform poorly are the models that undergo state collapse. (f) (total ESS)/(total TSS) vs
1996
       accuracy across featurizers. (g) ESS vs accuracy across featurizers. Note that without normalizing
       by kv (i.e. the task memory), the correlation with accuracy breaks down substantially. (h) TSS vs
1997
       accuracy across featurizers.

2359
2360
       Figure 23: Compression results. Note that ESS here refers to entropy-ESS and we abbreviate vocab
2361   size as vs in plots above. (a) ESS/vs vs accuracy across featurizers. (b) (total ESS)/vs vs accuracy
2362   across featurizers. (c) TSS/vs vs accuracy across featurizers. (d) (total TSS)/vs vs accuracy across
2363   featurizers. (e) ESS-accuracy correlation computed over the course of training in (TSS, kv) buckets.
2364   (f) ESS-accuracy correlation computed over the course of training in (total TSS, kv) buckets.
2365
2366   We note that with respect to the cross task-model trends, we find that in both selective copying and
2367   compression, task-adjusted ESS is a better proxy for model performance than task-adjusted TSS
2368   (Figures 22a, 22c, 23a, 23c). This is substantial as it demonstrates the utility of the ESS metric
2369   beyond just MQAR.
2370   Regarding within task-model trends, we observe similar patterns for selective copying as those seen
2371   in MQAR (Figure 22e), with one notable distinction. Namely, ESS and accuracy are positively
2372   correlated across a larger portion of the task-model space in selective copying than in MQAR. For
2373   compression, however, the within task-model trends look a bit different from what we observe in
2374   selective copying and MQAR (Figure 23e). One potential reason for this is that the compression
2375   task is significantly more difficult than the MQAR and selective copying tasks (as noted by the



                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   44
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


2376
       lower accuracies in Figure 23a), leading to more instabilities over the course of training. But in any
2377   case, this does highlight the fact that the strength of ESS as a proxy for model performance changes
2378   as a function of the task. The precise nature of this relationship is something we hope to explore in
2379   future work.
2380
2381   E.1.5   ESS T RAINING DYNAMICS IN MQAR
2382
2383   As mentioned in Section D.3, we observe a phase at the start of training in MQAR in which ESS
2384
       tends to decrease. This is shown in Figure 24 in which we select an arbitrary task-model configu-
       ration from the sweep and plot its ESS and accuracy over the course of training on a per featurizer
2385
       basis.
2386
2387                                                   Normalized Entropy-ESS                             Normalized Tolerance-ESS                    Accuracy
2388                                         SA                                 GLA                                LA                                 WLA
                                  1.0                                 1.0                               1.0                                 1.0
                   Metric value


                                                       Metric value


                                                                                         Metric value


                                                                                                                             Metric value
2389
2390                              0.5                                 0.5                               0.5                                 0.5
2391
2392                              0.0                                 0.0                               0.0                                 0.0
                                        0         50                        0       50                        0         50                        0       50
2393                                        Epoch                               Epoch                             Epoch                               Epoch
2394
2395   Figure 24: Training dynamics of ESS in select models (dmodel=256, heads=8) trained on MQAR
2396   (seqlen=2048, kv=64). We min-max normalize the ESS curves over the course of training to em-
2397   phasize the shape of the curve as opposed to its magnitude. Note that the tolerance-ESS shown here
2398   is computed using a tolerance of 1e-3.
2399
2400   We find that at the start of training (i.e. in between epochs 0 and 10), even if the accuracy is not
2401   evolving, the ESS is. In particular, in the recurrent frameworks (GLA, LA and WLA), we note
2402   a sharp decrease in the ESS before it begins to rise later in training (and along with it the model
2403
       accuracy). In contrast, in SA we observe the opposite: a sharp increase at the start of training
       followed by a steady decrease (even after it has solved the task). This points to a level of nuance in
2404
       the training dynamics of MQAR ESS that we have yet to characterize and is something we hope to
2405
       explore in future work.

       E.3   M ID -T RAINING A NALYSIS
2478
2479
2480   First, we provide some additional commentary on the ESS-based regularization results discussed in
2481   Section A. Recall we showed that decaying the A matrices in GLA and WLA towards the identity
2482   matrix enables these models to outperform LA in the state collapse regime. Our intuition for this
2483   result is that by ameliorating state collapse, GLA and WLA can better leverage their increased
       expressivity, which stems from their learnable A matrices – a feature absent from LA.


                                                                46
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


2484
                                                       Normalized Entropy-ESS                                      Normalized Tolerance-ESS                         Accuracy
2485
                                             SA                                      GLA                                         LA                                 WLA
2486                              1.0                                 1.0                                        1.0                                      1.0


                   Metric value


                                                       Metric value


                                                                                                  Metric value


                                                                                                                                           Metric value
2487
2488                              0.5                                 0.5                                        0.5                                      0.5
2489
                                  0.0                                 0.0                                        0.0                                      0.0
2490                                    0         50                           0          50                           0              50                        0       50
2491                                        Epoch                                    Epoch                                     Epoch                                Epoch
2492
2493
       Figure 27: An example of the training dynamics of ESS in select models (dmodel=512, heads=4)
       trained on MQAR (seqlen=2048, kv=128) that undergo state collapse (i.e. GLA and WLA). We
2494
       min-max normalize the ESS curves over the course of training to emphasize the shape of the curve
2495
       as opposed to its magnitude. Note that the tolerance-ESS shown here is computed using a tolerance
2496   of 1e-3.
2497
2498   Next, as mentioned in Section D.5, we provide some intuition behind the efficacy of regularizing
2499   only the second layer of the network as opposed to the first or both layers.
2500
2501
                                                                                             GLA Layer 0                    WLA Layer 0
2502                                                                                         GLA Layer 1                    WLA Layer 1

2503
2504                                                                           0.8
                                                                      ESS/kv


2505
2506                                                                           0.5
2507
2508                                                                           0.2
2509
2510                                                                                 26      27       28               29      210 211
2511                                                                                                seq len
2512
2513   Figure 28: Per-layer ESS/kv as a function of MQAR sequence length for the GLA and WLA featur-
2514   izers. ESS shown here is computed using a tolerance of 1e-3. Layers are 0-indexed.
2515
2516   Using 0-indexing for the layers, Figure 28 shows that layer 1 realizes a lower ESS/kv than layer
2517   0, particularly in the case of WLA. This suggests that layer 1 contributes disproportionately to the
2518   observed state collapse (Figure 27); consequently, it makes sense that layer 1 would need to be
2519
       regularized more heavily. Now, this begs the question as to why only regularizing the second layer
       leads to better performance than regularizing both layers (results of which were not shown). We have
2520
       two possible hypotheses for this outcome. First, introducing regularization terms for both layers may
2521
       complicate optimization by creating potentially conflicting objectives. Second, excessive decay of
2522   the A matrices towards the identity matrix may cause the model to revert to the LA regime, which
2523   – as shown in Figure 8b – performs worse than GLA and WLA (when sufficiently regularized).
2524   Nonetheless, we hope to further explore this intuition and investigate other ESS-based forms of
2525   regularization in future work.

2640
2641   Figure 32: All results presented here are computed using tolerance-based ESS with a tolerance set
2642   at 1e-1. Network layers are 0-indexed. (a) Per-layer ESS of all possible 4-layer GLA-SA hybrid
2643   networks. Experimental settings can be found in Section D.7. (b) Per-layer ESS of all possible 8-
2644
       layer GLA-SA Jamba-inspired hybrid networks. Experimental settings can be found in Section D.7.
       (c) Model accuracy and max/average ESS of SA layers in the 4-layer GLA-SA hybrid networks. (d)
2645
       Model accuracy and max/average ESS of GLA layers in the 4-layer GLA-SA hybrid networks.


                                                                                                                                   49
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


2646
       Recall in Section 5.2, we demonstrated an application of post-training ESS analysis through the lens
2647   of model distillation. Here, we provide another example of post-training analysis that leverages ESS,
2648   this time in order to gain intuition into learned network dynamics – hybridization. Hybridization is
2649   the process of arranging different operators in a multi-layer sequence model (Lieber et al., 2024;
2650   Glorioso et al., 2024; De et al., 2024). Specifically, we measure the per-layer ESS across various
2651   hybrid networks and find that the precise ordering of layers significantly influences ESS dynamics,
2652   offering intuition as to why certain hybrids outperform others.
2653   In this section, we present results from a post-training ESS analysis applied to GLA-SA hybrid
2654   networks to demonstrate the ability of ESS to capture differences among hybrid networks with
2655   varying topologies. In the first experimental setting, we train all possible 4-layer GLA-SA hybrid
2656   networks and compute the per-layer ESS on each model. We use the tolerance-based ESS since we
2657   want to analyze failure modes of learning in hybrid networks. In Figure 32a, we first note that in the
2658   pure GLA model, many of the layers fail to learn expressive states (as evidenced by the tolerance-
2659   ESS being 0), offering intuition as to why the model performs so poorly. Moving on to the hybrid
2660   networks with a single attention layer, we note that all of them perform quite well, except for the
2661
       network that has attention in the first layer. Interestingly, we find that when attention is placed as
       the first layer, it suffers from state collapse. At a higher level, this substantiates why many state-
2662
       of-the-art hybrid networks (such as Jamba) do not place attention as the first layer of the network.
2663
       However, such hybrids are typically constructed purely on the basis of performance: here, ESS is
2664   able to provide a distinct perspective. Next, examining the hybrids with 2 SA layers, we find that
2665   the only poor performing topology is with attention placed in the second and third layers. Again, we
2666   find that the ESS of the attention layers is lower than what we observe in the hybrids that solve the
2667   task, indicating its usefulness as a proxy for performance beyond the 2-layer non-hybrid networks
2668   we explored in Section 4.
2669
       To clarify this, we examine the maximum/average ESS (computed across layers) of the SA and
2670   GLA layers separately to understand how each relates to model performance. Notably, we find that
2671   maximum ESS across attention layers best correlates with accuracy (Figure 32c). Interestingly, the
2672   average SA layer ESS is a worse proxy for performance, potentially indicating that having a single
2673   layer with high memory utilization in hybrid networks is more important than having many layers
2674   with lower memory utilization. This offers support as to why hybrid networks like Jamba have a
2675   1:7 ratio between attention and non-attention layers. Regarding the GLA layers, we find that despite
2676   both the maximum and average SS varying across models, they do not correspond to changes in
2677   accuracy. One possible explanation for this is that since the attention layers are responsible for
2678
       driving the total ESS of the network up due to their unbounded state size, the role of non-attention
       layers in hybrid networks may not be captured entirely by the magnitude of their ESS. Nonetheless,
2679
       this is something we hope to explore in future work.
2680
2681   In the second experimental setting, we move beyond 4-layer GLA-SA hybrids to 8-layer GLA-SA
2682   hybrids. Here, instead of iterating over all possible topologies, we restrict the space of networks to
2683   those constructed via the hybridization policy proposed by Jamba. The Jamba hybridization policy
2684
       takes in the number of layers as input and provides a particular hybrid topology as output (refer to
       Lieber et al. (2024) for more details). Since most topologies explored in the 4-layer setting solved
2685
       the task, we both reduce the model dimension of the network and make the task more difficult to
2686
       see if we can observe performance differences across the architectures (model settings can be found
2687   in Table 8). Unsurprisingly, we find that the pure GLA network is unable to solve the task and also
2688   realizes a tolerance-based ESS of 0 in all layers (Figure 32b). However, more interesting is the fact
2689   that while the 2 SA-layer Jamba hybrid partially learns the task, the 3 SA-layer does not. Examining
2690   the ESS shows that the attention layers in the 3 SA-layer hybrid suffer from state collapse, which
2691   we know is highly correlated with poor performance on MQAR. This points to a deficiency of
2692   fixed-topology hybridization policies like Jamba which do not take into account factors like network
2693   trainability which can significantly influence model performance. Furthermore, this suggests that
2694   the ESS metric can be used to better inform the construction of hybrid networks. We hope to further
2695
       elucidate these per-layer ESS trends and leverage these insights to construct novel ESS-informed
       hybridization policies in future work.
2696

2807
       The figures above reveal significant cross-architectural differences in context processing. The
       attention-based model at a similar 7B scale (Figure 37, 38, and 39) shows minimal change in its


                                                                                        52
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


2808
       ESS pattern when an EOS token is replaced with a period (”.”). In contrast, the limited cache-size
2809   state-space model (Falcon Mamba 7B, Figure 7a) exhibits a substantial reduction in state modulation
2810   under the same token substitution.
2811
2812
       We attribute this difference to a phenomenon we term “preemptive state modulation” in limited state-
       size models, which stems from fundamental architectural differences. State-space models (SSMs)
2813
       with limited cache must efficiently manage their finite memory capacity and learn to preemptively
2814
       modulate state-size to optimize information retention, relying on explicit signals like EOS tokens to
2815   trigger context resets. In contrast, attention models with linearly increasing cache can store all past
2816   information without the need for selective forgetting, do not require preemptive state modulation,
2817   and show less sensitivity to explicit demarcation tokens. This distinction highlights the different
2818   strategies employed by various model architectures in managing context across diverse inputs, po-
2819   tentially influencing their performance on tasks requiring long-range recall or context separation.
2820
       However, a subset of attention models demonstrated varying state modulation patterns in response
2821   to different separator tokens, with this effect being more pronounced in smaller model sizes (see
2822   Figure 34, 35, and 36). This phenomenon, while not consistent across all attention architectures,
2823   merits deeper exploration.
2824
       Figure 40 illustrates the state modulation patterns at different tolerance levels for the four 1B lan-
2825
       guage models (LA, WLA, GLA, SA), trained under identical conditions.
2826
2827                                      ×106         Separator Token vs. ESS (tol=1.0e-03)                                                   ×106         Separator Token vs. ESS (tol=1.0e-02)
                                      5
2828                                             LA        GLA                           <sep>: <eos>                                   2.5           LA        GLA                          <sep>: <eos>
         Total Effective State Size


                                                                                                           Total Effective State Size

                                      3

2832                                  1                                                                                                 0.5

2833                                  0                                                                                                 0.0
                                          Context 1          Context 2              Context 3                                                  Context 1           Context 2             Context 3

2846
       Figure 40: An illustration of the effect of different separator tokens over different layers across dif-
       ferent tolerances. Softmax attention exhibits the most pronounced state modulation, beginning at a
2847
       tolerance level of 1e−2, followed by gated linear attention with significant modulation starting at a
2848
       tolerance of 1e−1. Weighted linear attention shows minimal modulation, only detectable at a toler-
2849   ance of 1.0, while linear attention displays no discernible separator token-induced state modulation.
2850   Here ESS is summed across channels and layers.
2851
2852   Notably, GLA exhibits a substantial variation in state modulation depending on the separator token,
2853   consistent with our earlier observations in Falcon Mamba regarding preemptive state modulation. In
2854   contrast, SA shows a smaller, yet non-trivial, effect. WLA and LA show no discernible differences
2855   across separator tokens, which may be attributed to their overall limited ability to modulate state
2856   size.

2866        Beyond sentence delimiters such as periods and end-of-speech tokens (discussed in Section 5.3), we
2867        observe similar “dips” in effective state-size where there are scope delimiter tokens such as “}”.

2869        inverse square root algorithm and a Fibonacci sequence generator algorithm.

        1   #include <iostream>
2888    2   #include <cmath>
2889    3
2890    4 // Quake Fast Inverse Square Root function
2891    5 float quakeFastInvSqrt(float number) {
2892
        6     long i;
        7     float x2, y;
2893    8     const float threehalfs = 1.5F;
2894    9
2895   10         x2 = number * 0.5F;
2896   11         y = number;
2897
       12         i = *(long*)&y;                                                     // Bit-level hacking: convert float to
                  long
2898   13         i = 0x5f3759df - (i >> 1);                                          // Initial magic number and bit shift
2899   14         y = *(float*)&i;                                                    // Convert back from long to float
2900   15

2901   16         // Newton’s method step for refining the result
2902
       17         y = y * (threehalfs - (x2 * y * y)); // First iteration
       18
2903   19         return y;
2904   20   }
2905   21

2906   22   int main() {
2907
       23       float number;
       24
2908   25         // Input: Get the number from the user
2909   26         std::cout << "Enter a number: ";
2910   27         std::cin >> number;
2911   28

2912
       29         // Output: Display the result using the Quake fast inverse sqrt
       30         float quake_result = quakeFastInvSqrt(number);
2913   31         std::cout << "Quake Fast Inverse Sqrt: " << quake_result << std::endl
2914              ;
2915   32
       33         // Compare with standard sqrt function


                                                                                            54
            Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


2916
       34        float std_result = 1.0f / std::sqrt(number);
2917   35        std::cout << "Standard Inverse Sqrt: " << std_result << std::endl;
2918   36
2919   37        return 0;
2920   38   }

2957
            E.6.2   H OW THE N UMBER OF P ROMPTING S HOTS A FFECTS THE E FFECTIVE S TATE -S IZE OF
2958
                    L ANGUAGE M ODELS
2959
2960
            Here, we explore how varying the number of shots when prompting large language models affects
2961
            their effective state-size patterns. We use Phi-2 as the candidate attention model and Mamba-2.8B
2962
            as the state-space model. The task we tested this on is MMLU (elementary mathematics).
2963
2964        At the start of the Q&A section for the attention model, there is a noticeable difference in state size
2965        between 0-shot and 1-shot prompts. Beyond 1-shot, the difference in ESS appears minimal. For the
2966
            state-space model, varying the number of shots has minimal impact on the effective state-size.
2967        Although these sparse experimental results require further investigation, we note the stark differ-
2968        ence in the effective state-size patterns between these two architectures, which provides additional
2969        insights into understanding the fundamental differences in the way prompts are processed across
            models.

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025
